message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>At present, there is a global pandemic of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pneumonia. For COVID-19 patients, cancer is a coexisting disease that should not be Here, we conducted a multicenter retrospective study to show the clinical information and outcomes of cancer patients infected with COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Measurements</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Medical records of COVID-19 patients with cancer admitted to hospitals from Jan 5, 2020 to Feb 18, 2020 were collected.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Of the 67 patients (median age: 66 years), the median age of patients with severe illness was older than that of patients with mild symptoms (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.001). The proportion of severe patients had co-morbidities was higher than patients with mild disease (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.004). During the treatment of COVID-19 pneumonia, tumor progression and recurrence was not observed for those patients still at the anticancer treatment phase. Lymphocytopenia was the main laboratory finding accompanying increased C-reactive protein and procalcitonin in cancer patients, especially in severe cases. By Mar 10, 2020, 18 (26.9%) patients died from COVID-19. The median age of survivors was younger than that of deaths (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.014). Lung cancer (n=15, 22.4%) was the most common cancer type and accounted for the highest proportion COVID-19 resulted deaths (33.3%, 5/15). We observed a tendency that patients at the follow-up phase had a better prognosis than that at anticancer treatment phase (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.095).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study showed COVID-19 patients with cancer seem to have a higher proportion of severe cases and poorer prognosis. We should pay more intensive attentions to cancer patients infected with COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: A Multicenter Study of Coronavirus Disease 2019 Outcomes of Cancer Patients in Wuhan, China

reference.unstructured: World Health Organization. Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) Situation Report - 63. https://www.who.int/docs/default-source/coronaviruse/situation-reports/20200323-sitrep-63-covid-19.pdf?sfvrsn=d97cb6dd_2 on 24 March 2020
